speaker: interviewer
text: 1995. And it's tape six of Max Durst's interview. You were--

speaker: interviewee
text: We are now free people in Ebensee. The war is over. I am-- due to my partial knowledge of English, I am now working with the American Army helping along, being a partial interpreter, helping along in the field hospital, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] and making acquaintances. Some soldiers, as a matter of fact, one particular soldier comes to my mind. His name is Private Joseph Rauchy. I befriended him. He was part of the American field hospital staff. And one day we are sitting and talking. And he is a very sympathetic young man. And he looks around. And he has his army coat. And he sees his army coat is missing, no army coat.

speaker: interviewer
text: He says, my god, what happened to my army coat? And from the corner of my eyes, I noticed that one of the fellow inmates that I-- I didn't know him, but I sort of knew him by sight-- walked away with a coat. It looked something like an American green army coat. I said, oh, my god, I think I  know what happened to your coat. I says, stay right here. I ran over. And I bumped into another friend of mine. And I said, do you remember this and this fellow? Somehow he must've taken this soldier's that I know, his coat. He says, yeah, he's in that barrack over there. So I ran over there. Sure enough, he's there with the coat. I said, look, these are the soldiers that liberated us. How can you can take his coat? Oh, he doesn't need it. What do you mean he doesn't need it? He has to buy a new one. He can't-- give it back. He says, all right, all right. I give it back. So I grab that coat. And I run back. And I said, oh, my god. You are an angel. You got my coat back. I'll never forget you. We became friends. He gave me his picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he, he, wrote, wrote down his name and his address in Staten Island. In fact, I still have his picture. I never got in touch with them somehow because I forgot all about it. And the other day when I looked through my pictures, after 50 years, I found the picture of Joe Rauchy. And I remember Joe Rauchy. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll look at that picture together.

speaker: interviewee
text: We'll look at that picture, yes. Now, shortly-- I would say that we were-- after liberation, we were-- and by the way, I must point out one thing to you which is rather important. The American doctors and the staff of that hospital warned us inmates. He says, look, you are now free. You can go out. You go out to the farms. You will see butter, and eggs, and all the goodies. He says, do not eat anything that your stomach is not conditioned for. You can eat bread. You can eat hard boiled eggs . Do not eat any heavy amounts of butter. Do not eat ham. Do not eat bacon because you will get sick. And it might be fatal because, you know, your stomach is conditioned-- is not conditioned to all this fatty food. Now, unfortunately, many, many of our people disregarded this warning. They went wherever they could. They grabbed. They ate. They got sick. They died. They could not be saved. And many, many people died. They lived-- they survived the war. But they died after the war because of all this fatty diet that, that they haven't seen for the last three years or thereabouts. And I just can't understand that they lived so long, and they lived through the war, and somehow, they couldn't resist it. And they just, just couldn't resist the butter, and the fat, and the bacon, and whatever else they had there. And they just couldn't. Now, they were warned-- we were-- this was something which was repeatedly warned. Loudspeakers warned it. Signs warned it. They personally came across the prisoners, actually the former inmates, and they were warned. They says, eat bread and hard-boiled eggs. Stay away from fat because this is poison for you.

speaker: interviewer
text: I notice that everything in your house is fat-free now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'm not so careful now. I'm not-- I'm not even careful now. I eat more than I should, fat. But it's a different situation now. Here, you're talking about not having any, any fat of any kind for three years, and then all of a sudden, you start eating this thing. And you see, we were-- they were in a run-down condition, and this contributed to them. Now, after being liberated three weeks later, or four weeks later, the Jewish Brigade from Palestine, which was part of the English Army, arrived at Ebensee. And they said, young people, whoever wants to go to Palestine, which was at that time Palestine, please join us. We will take you to Italy. And as soon as there's an opportune time, you will all immigrate to, to Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: So many, many of us went. We went on the trucks. They transported us to Italy. And partially by truck, partially by train, we ended up in the southernmost part of Italy-- the heel of the boot-- the famous heel of Italy. By the way, I must point out another thing. As we were traveling through Italy, they saw us-- the people on the trains were stopping or the trucks of the Jewish Brigade were stopping.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we were stopping in-- on stations, the Italian people were-- came running to us with bottles of wine and bread and fruit. They were extremely sympathetic to us. The Italian people were very, very nice, even though they may have been fascists, whatever they were. But they know-- they knew we were Jews because they said Hebreus, Hebreus, which means in Italian Jews. They brought us wine. They shared whatever. They themselves were poor because they were in a war-torn country. But they brought us-- they shared their bread with us. They shared their wine with us, the fruit. I mean, anything they had, they just gave us-- gave it to us. And they just couldn't do enough for us. So that is another incident which I'll never forget, is the kindness of the Italian people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there anything different about when the Czech women threw you the bread and the Italian people? Was there a difference there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there was a major difference. When the Italian people gave us food, there was no more danger because there were no Germans anymore. It was after the war. They shared with us the wine. Whatever meager possessions they had, they shared with us. The Czech people, they shared with us their meager possessions under the threat of a gun. Now, I mean, I cannot evaluate which one was more valuable. But I would say right now that they were both extremely, extremely kind and generous, both of them in their own way. So my heart goes out to the Czechs, and my heart goes out to the Italians. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I was wondering if maybe one kept you go and one gave you hope or if there was any sort of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this bread which was thrown at us in Czechoslovakia was probably more of a lifesaving gesture, while the wine and the other luxuries, they were already luxuries. At that time, we were no longer hungry. As I pointed out, the minute we were liberated, we never saw hunger because if it wasn't the American economy, it was the rations that the Americans brought us. It was no longer a matter of bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, it was a matter of getting fruit, which we didn't see for a long time. It was candy bars, and chewing gum, and whatever else we could get a hold of, you know? But they were both very kind. So this was-- this was again something which we don't forget-- which you don't forget easily.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, being in Italy, we ended up in a place called Santa Maria di Bagno, which was really a fascist resort-- a former fascist resort-- beautifully located right on the ocean. It was like-- we, we-- the villas that we got were really abandoned villas-- very primitive, but abandoned. And to they just had bare necessities with the UNRRA or the Jewish Brigade was able to secure for us-- sleeping accommodations and things like that. At that time, we already had some clothing change. We already got some things from the UNRRA, something that we could do with. Food was no problem. We had enough food because again, we were supplied by both the American and the-- and the Jewish Brigade. And we were there for, I would say, from somewhere around maybe July of 1945 until the winter of that same year, 1945. And then it became apparent that getting into Palestine wasn't going to be all that easy because the British closed the immigration. We heard about cases whereby any ship that came in was turned away because there was problems with the Arabs.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we decided to look. We would never be able to get to Israel. We might as well go back to a DP camp in Germany. And from there, we'll either register to go to Palestine, or we'll register to come to the United States. I mean we-- certainly, I didn't want to go back to Poland. I had absolutely no, no desire to go back to Poland. Poland did not bring back good memories. You told us that at 12, you looked forward to going to Palestine.

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: You told us that at 12--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --you would  look forward in your life to going to Palestine. And when you couldn't go--

speaker: interviewee
text: By the time I was 21, my outlook changed. Now, I became a little bit more materialistic, so to say. And I don't mind admitting it because this is what it is. I became more materialistic. And having the option to go to Palestine-- which at the time was closed anyway-- and having a possibility to come to the United States, I have chosen the latter rather than the former because here there was a very good possibility I would be able to immigrate to the United States within maybe a year or two. And Palestine was very uncertain.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we-- I decide-- we decided to go back to a DP camp because from Italy, there is no chance to get to United States. There were no DP camps. There was nothing there. So how do we go back, back to Germany? You make your way-- you have to go to the Austrian border-- Austria Italy, Italian border. That is near Bolzano. Bolzano is sort of northern-- part of Northern Italy.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the German side-- the Austrian side is Innsbruck. And what we did is we found our way on trains and so on and so forth. We didn't have any money, but we had ration cards, which were written in English, which was issued to us by UNRRA. Now, this is another comical story. We got on the train, and the conductor wanted tickets. We didn't have any tickets. So one of our-- of our group got the idea to give him-- show him the, the ration card, the meal ticket, that was written in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he looks at it. He says, this is not a ticket. He says, yes, this is a ticket. So we finally convinced him that this is a ticket. So the fellow-- I mean, the trains were crowded unbelievably. They were traveling on the roofs, and they were traveling on the steps. He punched the ticket. There were numbers-- one, two, three, four, meaning for the meals. So the first one, punched it.

speaker: interviewee
text: We came to the next train. He didn't want to-- the next conductor didn't want to punch it. So he says, well, the previous conductor punched it. You see, there has been a punch. So he punched it also. Now, that's how it was. There was one incident which I remember where one of the conductors flatly refused to punch it. So one of the fellows comes out. He looks at him. He says, hey, I remember you. You're a fascist. The guy looked at him. He says, give me the ticket. He quickly punched it. [LAUGHS] And chances are, he was a fascist, not that the fellow knew him. He wouldn't know him from Adam, but he didn't take any chances. He punched it. And that was that. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So we finally arrived at the-- at the Austro-Italian border. It was wintertime. Now, we have to go through-- we had to go to Germany. First, we have to get to Austria. So we got to Austria. How did we get to Austria? We had climb. We have to go through the mountains. Now, the mountains there are rough. They are huge mountains. We hire a guard.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had some-- we had a little, little money there. And we finally-- we chipped in, and we hired the guard. Now the guard was a-- was an Italian-- sort of an Italian German sort of, an older man. And he says, look, I will take you up to a certain point. And from there, you just strictly go downhill, and you end up in Austria. And he did. He was-- No sooner do we come on the Austrian side, we come across a French patrol. And we get caught by the French patrol. They want to see what we're doing there. And at first, they thought we were Germans trying to escape or whatever. So they took us all. They packed us in. And they took us to jail. They locked us in jail. I mean, overnight. They fed us. They gave us some food. And the next morning, they interrogated us. And as soon as they found out we are Jewish survivors of concentration camps, of course, they let us go. They didn't make any issues of it. They said, all right, you are free to go. Soon as they clear that up.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now, we have to make our way to the German border. So we traveled whatever way, whatever means we traveled there-- public transportation. We got to the German-- Austro-German border. Now, we have to cross over to Germany. Again, you have a border. You can't just go over from Austria to Germany. So we came across the American patrol. We see a border guard. The Americans are guarding the border.

speaker: interviewee
text: I am the official interpreter of my group. I go over to them and I say, look, we want to-- we have to go over to Germany. I gave him some kind of a story. My family is there. I'm looking for my family. I was in Italy. I came here. I says, look, you have no legal papers. I can't let you go to Germany. You can't. I just can't do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: We brought from Italy-- I remember, I had a sort of a satchel with oranges. So I take out a whole bunch of oranges. I say, I'll give you these oranges. Would you let us go? So he laughs. He says, I don't need your oranges. Save your oranges. I've got enough oranges. So I says, what's, what's the solution? He says-- so I said to him, how about-- so I asked him, do me a favor. Show me which side is already Germany. So he says, well, you see those trees in the forest there? As soon as you reach those trees, the other side is already Germany. And I said, well, supposing I go, and you don't know about it. You look the other way. So he says, well, look, as long as I don't see it. I don't see it. What I don't see, I don't see. I said, are you sure you're not going to shoot at us? He says, no, come on. I'm not going to shoot at you. So I said, all right, fellas, come. Let's go. I got a promise he won't shoot at us. So as soon as we went, he started. He got engaged in a conversation with another soldier. He looked the other way. And we-- of course, we got into Germany-- unofficially, but we got there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And now we were in Germany. And the next stop, we have to find the DP camp. We found out that there's a nearby DP camp in Austria. So I ended up in Fernwald. And I was in Fernwald from early 1946, which must've been around February 1946 until-- and then, of course, as soon as I had a chance, I registered with the American consulate to immigrate to the United States, which made it a little easier because I came under a certain youth quota. It was through the auspices of the actually American Joint Distribution Committee, which is an arm of the HIAS. And in 1947, which was about a little over a year later, I landed in New York in June 22, 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened? I did not have-- I-- we-- before we got on the boat, we got $15, $10 or $15, which was Coke money. [LAUGHS] So I arrived in the United States with $15 in my pocket, OK? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We were put up on-- in Manhattan on 103rd Street. There was a hotel called the Marseilles Hotel, which was specifically reserved for us young people that just arrived. There were other guests there too. But the, the HIAS reserved some room for us. And they gave us rooms. We had rooms there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, you know, in the evening, I'm standing in front of that hotel. And comes across a, a, a man and a lady. And they say, does any one of you know somebody by the name of Max Durst? I said, I am Max Durst. You're Max Durst? My name is Jack Manash. This is my wife Sally Manash. I said, you're Jack Manash? I knew them.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were-- you know, my uncle Jacob, his wife had a sister here in the United States. Her sister-- that sister and her husband were the ones that came. They saw on the list that there was a Max Durst on that list of arrivals. So they came looking for me. They were childless. They never had any children. So they, of course, immediately tried to accommodate me in any way they could. They helped me along. And, of course, we became very close for many years until they died.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they, they came. They visited us. And we became sort of family here, even though they were really not family because it was not-- it was through marriage, you know what I mean? It was a sister of my aunt by marriage. But in any event, they were very nice to us. So and again, this is another story, but I, I remember them quite well. How did you meet your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife? We were all newly arrived. We were-- we used to go stick together, like new immigrants usually do. And we organized dances. And one New Year's-- we used to have dances in different places. Now, one New Year's Eve dance was at the Hotel Diplomat. I don't know if it's still around, but it's in Manhattan on Times Square which was run by our own group here. And at that dance, I met my wife. And we sort of-- she was there with her cousin. I was there with another friend. And we sort of started dancing. And we sort of took a liking to each other. [LAUGHS] And we've been together ever since. We got married about-- let's see, now, we got married like this must've been-- this was New Year's Eve on December 31st, 1949. And I think we got married-- not I think. I better don't say I think. I'm going to get lynched later on by my wife. It was in-- it was, I think-- it was in August that same year that we got married in 1950.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was like nine months later that we got married. Was it 1949, or was it 1948 that we met? I have to-- I have to find out from Anna. I think it was-- it was in 1949. I know-- I know-- obviously, I know my marriage date. But I don't remember whether it was from '48 to '49 where we got-- where we-- where we met first, or was it '49?

speaker: interviewee
text: Let me think for one second. We knew each other year and a half-- a   year  and nine months. No, I think we knew each other nine months. It was nine months. It was-- it was '49-- '49 going into '50.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were you doing work-wise?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, I was working as a cabinet maker, working in the woodworking shop. And my basic training, which I received in the infamous Gorlitzer [? Holback ?] [? Holzbau ?] did come in a little bit handy because I had a basic knowledge of woodwork. And when I-- I started working-- by the way, when I arrived, a week later, I already had a job. I was working already. And it was through this person, that relative, the distant relative, who secured the job for me. He knew somebody had a woodworking shop, introduced me to him. I took the job. And I was working there. And I worked there until-- of course, we got married in August of 1950-- August 18, 1950. And I was working-- I continued working until late in 1951. By the late of 1951, [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Ann was already-- I think, we made a decision that I have to go into business for myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's no sense-- I mean, after all, I'm working for somebody a year and a half. How long can you work for somebody? You've got to be in business for yourself. So I made the decision to go. So I went out. And I went shopping for machinery. And whatever money we had, we bought-- I bought used machinery, old machinery. And my first shop was I rented a basement in Manhattan, which was sort of-- it wasn't a basement-- it was a basement, but it was an open basement.

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't even big enough for me to put a sign out. And I went to  a sign painter, painted signs, bought machinery, bought tools, and started making furniture. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] One of my first clients was a psychiatrist, who until even recently-- I, I spoke to him recently because he's sort of now retiring, and we spoke about he's moving to Florida and so on and so on. But and of course, I started building up a business from-- hand-- sort of-- it was a matter of word of mouth at the time. And until I built up a clientele-- and I've built up a considerable clientele over 40, 40-somewhat years. [LAUGHS] Some of my customers are quite noted people. Cyrus Vance is one of them, the former Secretary of State. I did work for, for-- he was a-- I did some works for people in show business. I did work for-- I'm talking about in the early days-- for a very famous singer, Fran Warren. I don't know if you've ever heard of her. She made-- this is before your days, but she made a record, of which is still being played now on the radio. She's still around. She plays nightclubs and so on. And I did work for political figures, you know, people in show business, people in, in the theater. I did work for the Imogene Coca in her day, you know, in her hey-day. But most of the time, I worked for people in the world of business, you know, people who, who can afford custom things. And then I branched out to other things. I branched out into real estate. I bought some buildings, which I own now. And I got involved in the stock market, which I'm still pursuing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I have my-- I have my hobbies, which I use-- these are really not hobbies. These are really things which I do to supplement things. How about your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: How about your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: My family? Well, you see, we were my oldest daughter Debbie-- our oldest daughter Debbie was born June 29, 1952. She's the first one. Then our second--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us a little bit about each kid.

speaker: interviewee
text: About each kid--

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you feel about them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Debbie, Debbie was cute as a button when she was born. She was just unbelievably cute. And of course, it's the first one that obviously is the cutest one. They're all cute, but the first one is always the cutest one. At that time, we lived in Brooklyn. We had an apartment in Brooklyn on Washington Avenue, which at that time was considered a good neighborhood. Today, it's not so hot anymore. But at that time, it was considered a nice neighborhood. And soon I think within a year or so, right shortly after she was born, we decided to rent for the summer a bungalow in Lake Oscawana. So Ann and the baby, Debbie, spent the summer there. And I used to join them weekends, you know, because I couldn't have-- I couldn't do anything else. I had to work during the week.

speaker: interviewer
text: So by the way, Ann also worked. She worked until-- in fact, she worked until Debbie was born. I think she worked until about the last month. She worked in a tie factory. She was a forelady in the tie factory. And she might have told you this. But weekends, I used to come out on Friday and leave Sunday morning.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, furthermore in 19-- later on, we decided to have another child. And Sharon was born. She was also cute as a button. [LAUGHS] But she was a little smaller. Debbie was a little bigger. And then, of course, a number of years later, our youngest son was born, David. After two girls, the boy came.

speaker: interviewer
text: We have to change tapes. Great.
